body:
Hi Em,

Quick follow-up from our call Sunday. Pulled up my training log — I'm averaging 28 miles a week over the last month, which is fine, but the right hip flexor tightness I mentioned after Tuesday's long run is exactly the kind of thing I'd flag immediately in a patient and then personally ignore. Added a 10-minute hip mobility circuit before each run starting this week. Preventive maintenance.

Also went ahead and registered for the Ponte Vedra 10K in October. Flat course, manageable distance, and it gives me something on the calendar besides early cath lab slots. Karen's official take: 'a midlife crisis with better metrics.' She's not wrong.

Thought about what you said — if you want to run it together, the registration's still open. No pressure, but the offer stands.

Dad

Dad!

'Better metrics' — honestly that's the best possible spin on it, and I'm stealing that line.

Count me in on the 10K. I've been running three or four days a week but completely unstructured, so having an actual race date would force me to follow a real plan for once. Send me the registration link whenever you have it and I'll sign up this week before I talk myself out of it.

Also — I say this with love — please actually do the hip mobility work. We both know you would write a patient a very firm note about training through early warning signs and then go run eight miles on it anyway. Practice what you preach, cardiologist.

Talk soon,
Emily

Emily,

Following up on what we talked about Sunday. My right IT band has been flaring consistently around mile 4 on my Tuesday and Thursday runs — third week in a row now. Friday I had to cut the long run short at 6 miles, which I didn't love. I've been foam rolling but clearly that alone isn't cutting it.

You mentioned you'd look into some Jacksonville-based sports PT clinics that work with runners specifically. If you found anything, send it over. Ideally someone who does gait analysis — I suspect my hip drop is the actual culprit and I'd rather fix the root cause than just manage symptoms indefinitely.

No rush, but sooner is better. Tennis Tuesday and Wednesday and I'd like to not make this worse.

Dad

Dad,

Found three options for you. Top pick is Apex Sports PT on San Jose Blvd — they have a treadmill gait lab on-site and two of their PTs specifically list runners as a focus population. Reviews are strong and they take your insurance. Second option is JAX Running & Rehab downtown, smaller practice but the lead PT is a former collegiate distance runner. Third is the sports medicine group attached to Baptist, if you'd prefer a more clinical setting.

I'd go with Apex first. I can book the intake appointment for you if you want — just tell me which week works. Also found a runner-specific hip strengthening protocol from the British Journal of Sports Medicine that gets cited a lot for IT band syndrome; I can forward the PDF if useful.

Let me know on the appointment and I'll handle it.

Emily

Emily,

Apex looks right. Go ahead and book it — any morning slot works except Tuesdays and Thursdays when I'm running before rounds. Friday morning would actually be ideal.

And yes, send the PDF. IT band issues in distance runners almost always trace back to hip abductor weakness; I already suspect that's my story, but I'd rather walk in with the evidence in hand than rely on the PT to make the case. Old habit.

Done on my end — foam roller is benched until I know what we're actually dealing with. Thanks for tracking all this down. You're efficient.

Dad

Emily,

Following up on our call Sunday — you mentioned your left knee was still bothering you after your last two runs. At your mileage and pace, that's almost certainly IT band irritation, not structural. Classic presentation.

What I'd do: drop your weekly volume by 30% for two weeks, no back-to-back run days, and add hip abductor work three times a week. Clamshells, lateral band walks — unglamorous but effective. Ice post-run if it flares. If it's not resolving in two weeks, get it imaged. Don't push through it; that's how a nuisance becomes a problem.

I do this loop three mornings a week before rounds, so I'm not handing you theory. The body tells you things if you listen early enough.

Let me know how it responds. Love you.
— Dad

Dad,

Thank you for this — honestly more useful than the sports medicine rabbit hole I'd been falling down online. I've already moved Thursday's run to a rest day and did the hip exercises this morning. Humbling how much those expose.

Quick question: does it matter what surface I run on while I'm recovering? I usually do pavement but there's a dirt trail near my apartment. Figured I'd ask the cardiologist who moonlights as a running coach.

Also, Mom mentioned you're thinking about signing up for the Thanksgiving half in Jacksonville. Any chance that's still on the table? I was looking at it too.

Love you!
— Emily

Emily,

Softer surface is genuinely better while the IT band is irritated — less repetitive impact loading. The dirt trail is the right call. Stick to it until you're two full weeks symptom-free, then reintroduce pavement gradually.

And yes, the Thanksgiving half is on the table. Karen's already lobbying me to register before it sells out, which is her way of making a decision for me in advance. I've been running the bridge route Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday mornings to build the base back up after a rough stretch in May. Pace is honest, not impressive.

Run it with me if your knee cooperates. We can set a reasonable goal and actually stick to it — unlike that 10K in Savannah where you sprinted the last mile and refused to admit you were dying.

Love you.
— Dad

Dad,

The dirt trail has been great — three runs in and zero knee complaints so far. The hip work is already making a difference. I'll give it the full two weeks before I even think about pavement.

And I am absolutely running the Thanksgiving half with you. Tell Mom her lobbying campaign worked on both of us. I'll register this week.

For the record, I was NOT dying in Savannah. I was executing a strong finishing kick. Cardiologically speaking.

Love you — see you at Thanksgiving if not before.
— Emily

Hey Em,

Circling back on our match Thursday — you were reading my serve better by the third set. Either you've been practicing or I'm getting predictable. Probably both.

I looked at that racket you mentioned, the Head Boom Pro. Decent mid-plus frame, good for players transitioning to more topspin. If you're serious about it, come by the house this weekend and you can hit a few balls with mine to get a feel for the weight before you spend anything.

Karen and I are out Saturday morning (she's doing her pilates thing), but I'm free from noon onward. I've got a standing Thursday-Friday court block at Deerwood so we could also schedule a proper rematch — forehand to forehand, no mercy.

Let me know what works.

Dad

Dad,

Okay first of all — 'no mercy' from the man who let me win the second set in 2019 and still brought it up at Thanksgiving. Noted.

I'd love to swing by Sunday if that works better. Saturday is tight on my end. And yes, I want to try the racket before committing. Honestly I've been watching some YouTube breakdowns of topspin mechanics and I think my grip has been off for years, so that probably explains a few things.

Also — totally separate — I signed up for a beginner cardio tennis clinic at the JCA next month. You'd hate it (way too casual), but I figured cross-training is cross-training, right? Isn't that what you always say on your Tuesday runs nobody asked about?

Sunday works. I'll bring lunch.

Emily

Hi Emily,

Following up on the Gmail API integration you helped me set up two weeks ago. The OAuth flow authorized fine initially, but I'm getting a 401 on token refresh every time the script runs unattended overnight. I've confirmed the credentials file is in place and the scopes look right, but something is clearly off with the refresh token lifecycle.

Context on why this matters: Ravi and I are piloting an automated post-discharge outreach workflow for our HF patients — if they're not getting the 7-day callback prompt, they fall through. This is the piece holding up the whole pilot.

Can you take a look at the token config? I suspect it's a scope issue or the credentials were generated under the wrong account type. Let me know what you need from me.

Thanks,
Dad

Hi Dad,

Got it — I know exactly what's happening. You generated the OAuth credentials under a personal Google account rather than a Workspace service account. Personal account refresh tokens can expire if the app isn't verified or if there's no user activity for an extended period, which explains why it fails overnight.

Fix is straightforward: I've set up a proper service account in the Cloud Console with domain-wide delegation and scoped it to gmail.send and gmail.readonly only. New credentials.json is attached — replace your existing file and re-initialize the auth flow once. After that, the token refresh will run indefinitely without user interaction.

Also noticed your redirect URI in the script is localhost:8080 but the Console had localhost:8000 registered. Updated it to match. Run the auth flow fresh after swapping the file and you should be clean.

Let me know if it clears up. Tell Mom I'll call her this week.

Emily

Emily,

Done. Swapped the credentials file, reran the auth flow, and the overnight refresh is holding — ran three unattended cycles this morning without a single 401. Sent a test discharge prompt through the API and it landed in under four seconds. Ravi's going to be relieved; we can start the pilot cohort next week.

The localhost mismatch was a good catch — I'd have debugged that for another two hours. Whoever stood up the original project clearly wasn't paying attention to the Console config.

Appreciate the quick turnaround. See you Sunday — Karen's doing short ribs.

Dad

Emily,

Following up on the Google Calendar API integration we talked through last week. Ravi and I are still hitting the same OAuth write failure on the shared on-call calendar — reads authenticate fine, but event writes fail silently. I've gone through the scopes twice in the console and they look correct on paper, which tells me I'm missing something obvious.

I blocked off Friday 3–5 PM and forwarded you read access to the Coastal Cardiology Partners calendar. Deadline is real: we're presenting the updated on-call scheduling system to the partners group Monday morning. Let me know what you need from my end.

— Dad

Dad,

Got the access, thank you. Silent write failure with valid reads is almost always a scope mismatch — you likely granted `calendar.readonly` during the initial consent flow but never included `calendar.events`. Updating the config file alone won't fix it; the token itself needs to be re-issued with the correct scope.

Steps: revoke the existing token in the Google Cloud console, update your scope array to include `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events`, then re-run the OAuth consent flow. If Dr. Hussain's account is also in the integration, he'll need to re-authorize on his end too. I'll send you a corrected JSON config tonight.

— Em

Em,

Done. Revoked both tokens, updated the scope array with your exact string, ran consent on my account and forwarded Ravi the re-auth link — he confirmed his side is through. Tested with a arbitrary event on the on-call calendar and it populated correctly. So: that part works.

New problem. My personal calendar is bleeding into the shared practice view. My runs Monday through Wednesday and tennis Thursday and Friday are now visible to the fellows, which is not ideal. Is there a calendar-ID filter I can set on the API side, or do I need to move personal events to a separate Google account entirely?

— Dad

Dad,

No need for a second account — this is a straightforward fix. You're almost certainly syncing against `primary`, which pulls everything. Instead, pass the specific calendar ID for the Coastal Cardiology Partners calendar (find it under Settings → 'Integrate calendar' → Calendar ID). That scopes the sync to practice events only and keeps your tennis schedule appropriately classified.

Also recommend setting `showDeleted: false` and a `timeMin` bound so you're not dragging in historical noise. I'll annotate both into the config before I send the final version tonight. You'll be ready well before Monday. Tell Mom I said hi.

— Em

Hi Em,

Following up on the Google Drive API issue we talked about last week. I finally carved out time between cases this morning — still can't get the service account credentials to authenticate properly. The OAuth flow throws a 403 on every attempt, even after I regenerated the key and re-shared the folder with the service account email.

I'm trying to automate pulling our cath lab productivity reports into a shared folder for the practice. Ravi and Sarah are both waiting on this before we can finalize the Q3 dashboard. Not mission-critical, but I'd like it sorted before Friday — I've got back-to-back tennis matches and zero bandwidth after that.

Can you point me to what I'm likely missing? I followed the Quickstart docs but I suspect the scopes are wrong.

Dad

Hi Dad,

Got it — classic scope issue. When you initialized the Google API client, you need to include 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive' (full access) or at minimum 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file' if you're only touching files the service account created. A 403 almost always means the scope in your credentials JSON doesn't match what you're requesting at runtime.

Also double-check that the Google Drive API is actually enabled in your Cloud Console project — easy to miss if you set up the service account before enabling the API itself. Go to APIs & Services > Library and confirm it shows 'Enabled.'

Once those two are squared away it should authenticate cleanly. Send me the relevant code snippet if it's still throwing errors and I'll take a look tonight.

Good luck with tennis Friday — try not to pull anything.

Emily

Hi Em,

Following up on the Notion API integration we discussed last week. I got the integration token set up through the developer portal, but the database query I'm running against our procedure log keeps returning an empty results array. Filter syntax looks correct to me, but something's off.

I've got back-to-back cath cases Thursday, so I'm not free until Friday post-run. If you can just send me a corrected query block I'll test it myself — whichever's easier on your end.

One more thing: does the API support nested database relations? Dr. Hussain and I are both using the referring physician log and want the rollups to actually work across linked databases.

— Dad

Hi Dad,

The empty results issue is almost certainly a property type mismatch. Notion's API is strict about filter conditions matching the exact property type. If 'Procedure Date' is a Date property, the filter object needs a 'date' key — not 'text.' Corrected query block is pasted below. Swap it in and it should resolve immediately.

On nested relations: fully supported, but rollups across linked databases require that both databases share the same integration. My guess is Dr. Hussain's referring physician database doesn't have your integration token added under Connections yet. That's the usual culprit.

Friday call still works if you want to walk through anything live — I'm free after noon Eastern.

— Em

Em,

Filter fix worked on the first try. I should have caught the property type mismatch myself — that's what I get for debugging API calls between ablations.

Confirmed the referring physician database wasn't connected to the integration. Added the token, rollups are populating correctly. Dr. Hussain tested his side this morning and it's pulling through clean on his end too.

One remaining question: I want to automate a weekly procedure volume summary — query the week's data and push it to a summary page. Is that achievable through the API alone, or does it require a third-party layer like Make or Zapier?

Thanks for the patience. Karen says hi.

— Dad

Emily,

Following up on our call Sunday — you mentioned your left knee was still bothering you after your last two runs. At your mileage and pace, that's almost certainly IT band irritation, not structural. Classic presentation.

What I'd do: drop your weekly volume by 30% for two weeks, no back-to-back run days, and add hip abductor work three times a week. Clamshells, lateral band walks — unglamorous but effective. I do this loop three mornings a week before rounds, so I'm not handing you theory. The body tells you things if you listen early enough.

Let me know how it responds. Love you.
— Dad

Love you!
— Emily

And yes, the Thanksgiving half is on the table. Karen's already lobbying me to register before it sells out, which is her way of making a decision for me in advance. I've been running the bridge route Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday mornings to build the base back up after a rough stretch in May. Pace is honest, not impressive.

Run it with me if your knee cooperates. We can set a reasonable goal and actually stick to it — unlike that 10K in Savannah where you sprinted the last mile and refused to admit you were dying.

Love you.
— Dad

Dad,

The dirt trail has been great — three runs in and zero knee complaints so far. The hip work is already making a difference. I'll give it the full two weeks before I even think about pavement.

And I am absolutely running the Thanksgiving half with you. Tell Mom her lobbying campaign worked on both of us. I'll register this week.

For the record, I was NOT dying in Savannah. I was executing a strong finishing kick. Cardiologically speaking.

Love you — see you at Thanksgiving if not before.
— Emily

Done on my end — foam roller is benched until I know what we're actually dealing with. Thanks for tracking all this down. You're efficient.

Dad

Hi Em,

Following up on our tennis plans — you mentioned Tuesday or Wednesday worked for you this week. I'm clear from 1:30 onward on Tuesday after clinic, and Wednesday afternoon is open too. Deerwood finally fixed the net on court 3, so we have no excuses on either side.

Your mom says she might come watch, which means she will definitely come watch and offer commentary from a lawn chair. Fair warning.

Let me know which day. — Dad

Hey Dad,

Tuesday at 1:30 is perfect! I've actually been working on my backhand since the last time we played, so consider yourself warned. I'll bring water and sunscreen because it's July in Jacksonville and I have no interest in a heat emergency on a tennis court.

Tell Mom she's welcome to come — her commentary is honestly more entertaining than the match. See you Tuesday!

Emily

Em,

Tuesday 1:30. Done. I'll text if a case runs long — the cath lab occasionally has opinions about my afternoon schedule — but I'll be there.

Your backhand. I'll believe it when I see it. Also, bring electrolytes, not just water. Heat plus exertion in July is a real physiological problem, not a suggestion.

Your mom has already confirmed she's coming and already claims she hasn't decided yet. Classic.

Dad

Dad,

Noted on the electrolytes — grabbing Nuun tabs on my way over. And honestly, Mom's sideline commentary is its own sport at this point. Looking forward to it.

Try not to let the cath lab steal you away — I'll be warmed up and waiting. Loser buys dinner after?

See you Tuesday,
Emily
